label: Cautiously hopeful
summary:
On the second day of the G7 summit at Évian, the week's defining story moved from announcement toward execution. The United States and Iran had virtually signed their accord to end a three-month war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz - President Trump and Vice President JD Vance signing for Washington, Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf for Tehran - with a formal ceremony set for Friday, June 19 in Switzerland and a 60-day clock to start on Iran's nuclear file. Markets extended their relief rally: the Nasdaq surged 3.07%, the S&P 500 climbed 1.65% and the Dow added 0.92%, led by Boeing, Nvidia and Amazon, while Brent crude settled below $81 a barrel as traders waited for the oil to actually flow.

Diplomacy crowded the Évian agenda. Leaders held a special session on Ukraine with President Zelensky present; he revealed he had offered to meet Vladimir Putin on the summit's sidelines with Trump and Macron in the room, and said Europe and the US agreed but 'Russia has once again shown that it is not ready to engage in dialogue.' A Zelensky-Trump bilateral was expected the same day. The harder edges of the ledger held firm: Britain confirmed its armed forces had boarded and detained the sanctioned tanker Smyrtos in the Channel - the first UK-led seizure of a Russian 'shadow fleet' vessel - Israel kept striking Lebanon after excluding itself from the Iran deal, and the IPC's confirmed famine in Sudan's El Fasher and Kadugli ground on, with 4.2 million acute-malnutrition cases now projected for 2026 against a response plan funded at about 16%. Against that, recovery from the June 8 magnitude-7.8 Mindanao earthquake shifted fully from rescue to rebuilding as power and roads came back, the men's World Cup rolled through a packed June 16 slate across North America, and a pancreatic-cancer mRNA vaccine reported durable survival at the AACR meeting. A war edging toward signature and a second day of soaring markets keep the mood at the lower edge of good - held there, not higher, by an unsigned deal, a rebuffed peace overture, Israeli strikes and Sudan's famine.

drivers.headline: The U.S. and Iran virtually sign their accord to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz - Trump and VP Vance signing for Washington, Speaker Ghalibaf for Tehran - with a formal ceremony set for Friday, June 19 in Switzerland and 60 days of nuclear talks to follow
Wall Street extends its relief rally on the Iran deal: the Nasdaq surges 3.07%, the S&P 500 climbs 1.65% and the Dow adds 0.92% (up 469 points to 51,671), led by Boeing, Nvidia and Amazon
Brent crude settles below $81 a barrel as investors await details of the U.S.-Iran agreement that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz and resume Gulf oil flows once the deal takes effect

G7 leaders hold a special Ukraine session at Évian with President Zelensky present, as he reveals an offer - backed by the U.S. and Europe - to meet Putin on the summit's sidelines and a Zelensky-Trump bilateral is expected

Russia again rebuffs talks: Zelensky says 'Europe and the United States have agreed, while Russia has once again shown that it is not ready to engage in dialogue,' leaving the war without a ceasefire as the G7 meets

British armed forces board and detain the sanctioned tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel - the first UK-led seizure of a Russian 'shadow fleet' vessel - underscoring the maritime front of Moscow's war and sanctions evasion

Israel, excluded from the Iran deal, keeps striking Lebanon - expanding ground positions north of the Litani after killing at least 16 in Tyre and hitting Beirut's southern suburbs - keeping the Israel-Hezbollah front volatile

Sudan's famine grinds on: with IPC-confirmed famine in besieged El Fasher and Kadugli, the WFP warns 4.2 million acute-malnutrition cases are now projected for 2026 - including over 800,000 severe - against a response plan funded at about 16%

Recovery from the June 8 magnitude-7.8 Mindanao earthquake shifts fully from rescue to rebuilding as the grid operator restores all toppled transmission lines and most roads and bridges are cleared, even as at least 37 are confirmed dead

The men's World Cup rolls through a packed June 16 slate across North America - France-Senegal and Iraq-Norway in the U.S. Northeast, Argentina-Algeria in Kansas City, Portugal-DR Congo in Houston and Uzbekistan-Colombia in Mexico City
A personalized pancreatic-cancer mRNA vaccine reports durable results - nearly 90% of immune responders still alive up to six years out - in follow-up data presented at the 2026 AACR meeting
Clean-energy momentum holds as U.S. solar out-generates coal for a first full month (12.8% of power to coal's 12.2%) and Australia's emissions fall about 2% on a rooftop-solar boom

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Mindanao earthquake / 52nd G7 summit / 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis / 2026 FIFA World Cup)
